title: Chapter 19: The Confidante
content: The garden smelled of crushed thyme and cold stone.

Jane found her there — or rather, Blanche found her. It was past eleven, and the Loire night had settled over the château like a held breath, the air thick with the green decay of late summer and the mineral sting of dew on limestone. Jane had come outside to think, to escape the surveillance angles that tracked every corridor, every doorway, every shared glance inside Thornfield. She had not expected company.
But Blanche Ingram was standing among the dormant rose canes like a woman carved from the dark itself.

She had changed. That was the first thing Jane noticed — not the absence of Thornfield's geometric tailoring, though that was striking enough, but the way Blanche held her shoulders without it. The black dress was simple, almost severe: no shoulder pads, no architectural seaming, no label visible anywhere. Just matte fabric that absorbed the moonlight and gave nothing back. It could have been from any store in any city. It could have been borrowed, stolen, or dug out of the bottom of a suitcase. It fit Blanche the way a cast fits a healing bone — practical, uncomfortable, necessary.

Without the brand's scaffolding, Blanche looked smaller. Younger. Her posture had lost its engineered confidence, and what remained was something rawer: the curve of a spine that had bent under weight it was never designed to carry.
"You came," Jane said, though it wasn't a question.

"I almost didn't." Blanche's voice carried differently in the garden — no walls to bounce off, no acoustics to shape it. It sounded flatter. More honest. She didn't look at Jane; she looked at the château, its windows glinting amber above them. The surveillance cameras didn't reach this far into the garden. The rose canes and the old stone wall formed a blind spot, a pocket of unmonitored space that Jane had mapped in her first week. Blanche had found it too. Of course she had. She'd been living inside this machine longer than anyone except Grace Poole.

"I leave tomorrow," Blanche said. "The car's coming at seven. I'll be in Paris by noon, and by Monday I'll be someone else's project." She paused. "But I couldn't go without — " She stopped. Her jaw worked, the muscle jumping beneath skin that had been scrubbed free of the foundation she'd worn every day at Thornfield. Without it, the shadows under her eyes were visible. Bruise-dark. Real.
"Without what?" Jane asked.
Blanche turned to face her then. The garden's darkness made her features softer, almost indistinct — a stranger who happened to be wearing Blanche Ingram's face.
"Without telling you what I know."

The night pressed in. Somewhere beyond the garden wall, the river moved, slow and indifferent. Jane could taste the damp on her lips, could feel the chill lifting from the ground through the thin soles of her shoes. She waited.

"There were rumors," Blanche began. She spoke the way a person speaks when they've rehearsed something so many times the words have worn smooth. "Before I came to Thornfield. After, too — at the parties, the shows. People drink at those things, and they talk. There was always this — undercurrent. About Edward." She wrapped her arms around herself, the black fabric pulling tight across her elbows. "A brilliant designer. A woman. She worked with him in the early years, when Thornfield was still establishing itself. The first collection — the one that won the Prix Médicis — people said it was hers. That Edward took credit for her construction, her patterning. That without her, there would have been no Thornfield."
Jane's pulse clicked up a notch, but she kept her face still. "What happened to her?"

"That's the part everyone whispers." Blanche's mouth twisted. "She 'had a breakdown.' That's the phrase they use. Like it was weather. Like it just happened to her. 'Did you hear about Edward's first designer? Such a shame. So talented. Such a tragedy.' That's how they talk about her. In the past tense. Like she's already dead." She swallowed. "I never saw her. I was here for two years and I never once saw her face. But I heard her."
The air changed. Jane felt it — a subtle shift, as though the garden itself had leaned in to listen.
"Late at night," Blanche continued. "The scissors."
The word landed in Jane's chest like a stone in still water. She kept her breathing even.

"I'd be in bed — his bed, eventually — and I'd hear them. This sound. Snick, snick, snick. Coming from below. From somewhere underneath the house." Blanche's voice had dropped to a whisper, as though the walls might still be listening, even out here in the blind spot. "I told myself it was mice. Old houses make noises. Pipes. Settling stone. I told myself that for months, because — " She broke off. When she spoke again, her voice was stripped raw. "Because if it was a woman, then what he's doing to me isn't love. It's practice."
The word hung between them. Blanche's eyes were dry — she'd cried herself empty already, Jane realized. There was nothing left but salt and exhaustion.

"I was the second prototype," Blanche said. "That's what I keep thinking. She was the first. He worked out the method on her, and then he refined it on me. The isolation. The — " She gestured vaguely toward the château. "The architecture. Making you dependent on the structure he builds around you. Making you think you can't stand without it."

Jane thought of her own engagement ring, the weight of it on her finger. She thought of the suite Edward had designed for her, every detail curated, every surface reflecting back the version of herself he wanted to see.

"There's something else," Blanche said. Her arms tightened around herself. The black dress creased at the elbows, fabric whispering against fabric. "Edward used to leave. Late at night. After I'd fallen asleep, or sometimes before — he'd just get up and go. Down the back stairs. To the basement."
Jane's hands were cold. She pressed them against her thighs, feeling the denim stiff and rough beneath her palms.

"I assumed it was an affair," Blanche said. Her laugh was a short, ugly thing — a bark with no humor in it. "Another woman. Someone younger, probably. Prettier. I'd lie there in his bed and think about who she might be. What she looked like. What she had that I didn't. I'd rehearse confrontations. I'd plan what I'd say when I found her." She shook her head slowly. "I was jealous of a prisoner, Jane. That's how sick this is. I competed with a woman who couldn't leave, and I still lost."

The silence that followed was enormous. It swallowed the garden, the château, the sky. Jane could hear her own heartbeat in her ears, could smell the metallic edge of the river and the faint sweetness of the roses dying on their canes. She could feel the cold rising through the soles of her shoes, the ground beneath her feet solid and ancient and laced with secrets.
"How often?" Jane asked. Her voice was steady. Clinical. "The visits. How often did he go down?"
Blanche looked at her — really looked at her, for the first time since they'd stepped into the garden. Something flickered across her face. Recognition, maybe. Or the beginning of it.

"Twice a week. Sometimes three times. Always after eleven. Sometimes he'd be back in an hour; sometimes I'd wake at two and he'd still be gone. For — " She hesitated. "For three years. It was consistent. Like a schedule."

Jane's mind was already moving, already laying the pattern out like a blueprint. Twice weekly. Eleven PM to two AM. Three years. That wasn't an affair — affairs were irregular, driven by desire and opportunity and the chaos of two lives colliding. This was a roster. A visiting schedule. The careful, sustained attention of a man maintaining something he considered his.
She looked at Blanche and saw, with terrible clarity, not a rival, not a foil, not the woman Edward had nearly married. She saw a witness.

Blanche Ingram had chosen not to see. That was the crucial thing. She'd heard the scissors and called them mice. She'd noticed the absences and called them infidelity. She'd lived inside the architecture and told herself it was shelter. But her choice meant she had heard things, seen patterns, noted absences. She knew when Edward left and when he returned. She knew the rhythm of his disappearances, the frequency, the duration. She knew the sound of scissors cutting fabric in the dark — and she could identify it, under oath, as human. Not mice. Not pipes. Not settling stone.

Blanche's testimony wouldn't just be about personal abuse. It would place Edward at the basement door at specific times with specific patterns. It was corroboration. Independent, unwilling, undeniable corroboration from a woman who had every reason to hate the person she was corroborating.

Jane watched Blanche's face as understanding rearranged her features. The recognition wasn't comfortable — it was the kind that comes with a price, the kind that demands you acknowledge what you've been complicit in.
"You're going to do something," Blanche said. It wasn't a question. Her voice was quiet but certain, stripped of its usual performance. "About her."
Jane said nothing. She didn't confirm. She didn't deny. The garden held its breath around them.
"Whatever it is," Blanche said, and her voice cracked on the first word, healed itself, and pressed forward — "when the time comes — I want to be there."

She stepped closer. The moonlight caught the damp on her cheeks, and Jane realized she was crying after all — not the theatrical tears of a woman performing grief, but the silent, involuntary kind. The kind that leaks out when the body knows something the mind hasn't accepted yet.

"Not for revenge," Blanche continued. "For the record. I want to be on the right side of something for once in my carefully curated life." She wiped her face with the back of her hand, the gesture rough and ungraceful. "I've spent twelve years in this industry letting people shape me into whatever they needed. Accessory. Muse. Brand vehicle. I've been Blanche Ingram™ for so long I forgot there was a person underneath. But she's down there, isn't she? The woman with the scissors. And she's been a person this whole time. She just wasn't allowed to be one."
The night pressed against them. The château's windows glowed above — warm, surveilled, complicit. Jane felt the weight of the building at her back, its stones soaked in years of silence.
"I'll contact you," Jane said. "When the time comes."

Blanche nodded once. Then she turned to leave, her black dress disappearing into the garden's shadows like ink dissolving in water. But she stopped at the edge of the rose bed, half-turned, the moonlight catching only the line of her jaw.
"The last thing he said to me," she said, "before the gift basket arrived — before the performance of his decency — he looked right at me and said, 'You've been invaluable, Blanche. Truly.'"
She let the word hang there. Invaluable.

"Not loved. Not missed. Not cherished. Invaluable. Like I was a seam he'd finished. A textile he'd exhausted. A resource extracted and accounted for." She shook her head, and the last of the Blanche Ingram that Jane had known — the armored, lacquered, Thornfield-grown version — fell away like a silk scarf slipping from a shoulder. What remained was a woman in a black dress, standing in a garden at midnight, choosing to walk toward something instead of away from it.
"Invaluable," she repeated, quieter now. "That's the word he used. I've been repeating it for three days and it keeps getting worse."
She left. Jane heard her heels on the gravel — different shoes, lower, quieter — and then the sound of the side door opening and closing. The garden was empty.

Jane stood alone among the dormant roses. The night air settled against her skin like a second garment, cool and thin, carrying the faint industrial ghost of the river and the green, dying smell of the garden at the end of its season. Above her, the château rose in its elegant geometry, every window a lens, every corridor a corridor of proof waiting to be walked.
She pulled out her notebook. The pen was cold in her fingers. She wrote quickly, the words small and precise in the moonlight:

*Blanche Ingram. Witness — not because she saw everything, but because she chose not to see, and now she's choosing otherwise. Pattern of visits: Edward to basement, 11 PM to 2 AM, twice weekly, for three years. That's not an affair. That's a visiting schedule. That's a kept person. Account: the scissors — identified by witness as human, not mechanical. Prior accounts at industry events reference the designer, designated as "breakdown," reputational salvage. Edward's framing: tragedy, not imprisonment.*
She underlined the last two words. Twice.
And then — faint, rising through the cold ground beneath her feet — she heard it.
Snick. Snick. Snick.

Scissors. The same steady, patient rhythm that had been cutting through the floors of this house for five years. The same rhythm Blanche had mistaken for mice, the same rhythm Jane had first heard on her second night, the same rhythm that had been threading its way through the maintenance logs and the gaps in the walls and the spaces between words.
The person behind them was still working. Still cutting. Still waiting.

Jane looked down at the earth beneath her shoes. The grass was wet, the soil dark and compressed. Below it, stone. Below the stone, a basement. Below the basement, a woman with fabric and shears and five years of patience, building something that couldn't be seen from the surface.
"I hear you," Jane murmured. The sound of the scissors didn't waver. It didn't need to. It had been patient long before Jane arrived; it would be patient long after.

She straightened. The coalition had its first external witness — a woman who had been inside the architecture and survived it, who could map the patterns from personal observation, who would stand up and say, under oath, *I heard her. I knew. And I chose, finally, to speak.*

And below her feet, in the dark, the scissors kept cutting. Someone was still at work. Someone was still waiting. And the thread they were sewing — patient, invisible, impossible to unpick — was finally, after five years, beginning to pull taut.
